tool_use.name: Edit
tool_use.param:
Conflict markers typically look like:
```python <<<<<<< HEAD from erk_shared.gateway.gith...[truncated 498 chars]...mport from erk_shared.gateway.github.parsing import parse_pr_number, parse_plan_number ```

Conflict markers typically look like:
```python <<<<<<< HEAD from erk_shared.gateway.gith...[truncated 559 chars]...erk_shared.gateway.github.parsing import parse_pr_number_from_url, parse_repo_from_url ```

1→"""Shared utilities for run commands.""" 2→ 3→import re 4→ 5→ 6→def extract_pr_number(display_title: str | None) -> int | None: 7→ """Extract PR number from display_title containing '#NNN'. 8→ 9→ Handles: 10→ - "pr-address:#456:abc123" → 456 11→ - "8559:#460:abc123" → 460 12→ - "one-shot:#458:abc123" → 458 13→ - "rebase:#456:abc123" → 456 14→ - "8559:abc123" (old format, no #) → None 15→ - "Some title [abc123]" (legacy) → None 16→ - None or empty → None 17→ """ 18→ if not display_title: 19→ return None 20→ match = re.search(r"#(\d+)", display_title) 21→ if match is None: 22→ return None 23→ return int(match.group(1)) 24→ 25→ 26→def extract_plan_number(display_title: str | None) -> int | None: 27→ """Extract plan number from display_title format '123:abc456'. 28→ 29→ Handles: 30→ - Legacy format: "123:abc456" → 123 (plan_id at start, no #pr_number)
... [19 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: "Runs Tab Architecture" 3→read_when: 4→ - "working with TUI Runs tab" 5→ - "adding columns to RunDataTable" 6→ - "understanding branch resolution in runs" 7→ - "modifying workflow run display in TUI" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "using run.branch directly for display without checking PR head_branch first" 10→ warning: "After merge+deletion, run.branch becomes master/main. Use PR head_branch as primary source, falling back to run.branch only if not master/main." 11→ - action: "adding a new column to RunDataTable without updating RunRowData" 12→ warning: "RunDataTable columns are populated from RunRowData fields. Add the field to RunRowData first, then add the column in _setup_columns." 13→--- 14→ 15→# Runs Tab Architecture 16→ 17→The Runs tab displays GitHub Actions workflow runs in the TUI, alongside Plans, Learn, and Objectives views. 18→ 19→## RunRowData 20→ 21→Frozen dataclass representing one workflow run row. 22→ 23→**Source**: `src/erk/tui/data/types.py` 24→ 25→| Field | Type | Description | 26→| ------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------- | 27→| `run_id` | `str` | GitHub Actions workflow run ID | 28→| `run_url` | `str \| None` | URL to the GitHub Actions run page | 29→| `status` | `str` | Raw status: "queued", "in_progress", "completed" | 30→| `conclusion` | `str \| None` | Raw conclusion: "success", "failure", "cancelled", None |
... [78 lines omitted] ...

1→"""Run table widget for TUI dashboard Runs tab.""" 2→ 3→from __future__ import annotations 4→ 5→from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, cast 6→ 7→from textual.events import Click 8→from textual.message import Message 9→from textual.widgets import DataTable 10→ 11→from erk.tui.data.types import RunRowData 12→ 13→if TYPE_CHECKING: 14→ from erk.tui.app import ErkDashApp 15→ 16→ 17→class RunDataTable(DataTable): 18→ """DataTable subclass for displaying workflow runs. 19→ 20→ Manages column configuration and row population from RunRowData. 21→ Uses row selection mode (not cell selection) for simpler navigation. 22→ """ 23→ 24→ class RunClicked(Message): 25→ """Posted when user clicks run-id column on a row.""" 26→ 27→ def __init__(self, row_index: int) -> None: 28→ super().__init__() 29→ self.row_index = row_index 30→
... [55 lines omitted] ...

session.tool_result: Todos have been modified successfully. Ensure that you continue to use the todo list to track your progress. Please proceed with the current tasks if applicable

1→"""Summarize implementation failures using Haiku. 2→ 3→Reads a raw session JSONL file, extracts the tail entries, sends them 4→to Claude Haiku for failure diagnosis, posts the summary as a PR comment, 5→and prints the summary to stdout for use as a GitHub Actions job summary. 6→ 7→Usage: 8→ erk exec summarize-impl-failure \ 9→ --session-file /path/to/session.jsonl --pr-number 42 10→ erk exec summarize-impl-failure \ 11→ --session-file /path/to/session.jsonl --pr-number 42 \ 12→ --exit-code 1 13→ 14→Output: 15→ Markdown failure summary to stdout (for GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY) 16→ 17→Exit Codes: 18→ 0: Always (diagnostic tool, never blocks workflow) 19→""" 20→ 21→from __future__ import annotations 22→ 23→from dataclasses import dataclass 24→from pathlib import Path 25→ 26→import click 27→ 28→from erk.artifacts.paths import get_bundled_github_dir 29→from erk_shared.context.helpers import require_cwd, require_prompt_executor 30→from erk_shared.learn.extraction.session_preprocessing import (
... [219 lines omitted] ...
75→ # Take the tail for failure diagnosis 91→def _build_failure_prompt( 97→ """Build the failure diagnosis prompt from template with variable substitution. 109→ template_path = prompts_dir / "prompts" / "impl-failure-summarize.md" 117→ f"Analyze this implementation session that failed with exit code {exit_code_str}. " 118→ f"What was the agent doing when it stopped? Did it encounter an error?\n\n" 124→ """Build PR comment body from failure summary. 136→ "## Implementation Failure Summary", 145→def _post_failure_comment(*, pr_number: int, comment_body: str, cwd: Path) -> None: 146→ """Post failure summary as a PR comment. 162→ operation_context=f"post impl failure summary on PR #{pr_number}", 168→@click.command(name="summarize-impl-failure") 178→def summarize_impl_failure( 185→ """Summarize an implementation failure using Haiku. 200→ minimal = "Session file is empty or not found — unable to analyze failure." 206→ _post_failure_comment(pr_number=pr_number, comment_body=comment_body, cwd=cwd) 212→ prompt = _build_failure_prompt( 230→ summary = "(Failure summarization was unable to produce a diagnosis.)" 240→ _post_failure_comment(pr_number=pr_number, comment_body=comment_body, cwd=cwd)

560→ cmd.append("--dangerously-skip-permissions") 561→ if system_prompt is not None: 562→ cmd.extend(["--system-prompt", system_prompt]) 563→ if tools is not None: 564→ cmd.extend(["--allowedTools", ",".join(tools)]) 565→ 566→ try: 567→ result = subprocess.run( 568→ cmd, 569→ input=prompt, 570→ capture_output=True, 571→ text=True, 572→ cwd=cwd, 573→ check=False, 574→ env=self._subprocess_env(), 575→ timeout=PROMPT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, 576→ ) 577→ except subprocess.TimeoutExpired: 578→ return PromptResult( 579→ success=False, 580→ output="", 581→ error=f"Prompt execution timed out after {PROMPT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS}s", 582→ ) 583→ 584→ if result.returncode != 0: 585→ return PromptResult( 586→ success=False, 587→ output="", 588→ error=format_prompt_error( 589→ returncode=result.returncode,
... [59 lines omitted] ...

session.assistant: Now let me check what workflow step uses this:
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: Subprocess Wrappers 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 00:00 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→read_when: 6→ - "using subprocess wrappers" 7→ - "executing shell commands" 8→ - "understanding subprocess patterns" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "using bare subprocess.run with check=True" 11→ warning: "Use wrapper functions: run_subprocess_with_context() (gateway) or run_with_error_reporting() (CLI). Exception: Graceful degradation pattern with explicit CalledProcessError handling is acceptable for optional operations." 12→ pattern: "subprocess\\.run\\(" 13→ - action: "adding a Claude subprocess call with --print mode" 14→ warning: "Always include --no-session-persistence flag and use env=build_claude_subprocess_env() parameter. Both are required to prevent session persistence and CLAUDECODE context leakage. See the 'Claude Subprocess Environment' section." 15→ - action: "using sed -i in scripts that run on both macOS and Linux" 16→ warning: "macOS sed requires `sed -i ''` (empty string argument) while Linux sed uses `sed -i` (no argument). Scripts that use sed -i without handling this difference will fail silently on one platform." 17→ score: 4 18→--- 19→ 20→# Subprocess Execution Wrappers 21→ 22→**NEVER use bare `subprocess.run(..., check=True)`. ALWAYS use wrapper functions.** 23→ 24→This guide explains the two-layer pattern for subprocess execution in erk: gateway layer and CLI layer wrappers. 25→ 26→## Scope 27→ 28→**These rules apply to production erk code** in `src/erk/` and `packages/erk-shared/`. 29→ 30→**Exception: erk-dev** (`packages/erk-dev/`) is developer tooling and is exempt from these rules. Direct `subprocess.run` is acceptable in erk-dev commands since they don't need the testability/dry-run benefits of wrapper functions.
... [348 lines omitted] ...
34→Erk uses a two-layer design for subprocess execution to provide consistent error handling across different boundaries: 36→- **Gateway layer**: `run_subprocess_with_context()` - Raises RuntimeError for business logic 37→- **CLI layer**: `run_with_error_reporting()` - Prints user-friendly message and raises SystemExit 47→**Behavior**: Raises `RuntimeError` with rich context on failure 49→**Usage**: Pass `cmd` (the command list), `operation_context` (human-readable description), and `cwd` (working directory). On failure, raises `RuntimeError` with the operation context, command, exit code, and stderr. 57→- **Rich error messages**: Includes operation context, command, exit code, stderr 58→- **Exception chaining**: Preserves original CalledProcessError for debugging 61→### run_with_error_reporting (CLI Layer) 63→**When to use**: In CLI command handlers where you want to immediately exit on failure with a user-friendly message. 65→**Import**: `from erk.cli.subprocess_utils import run_with_error_reporting` 67→**Behavior**: Prints error message to stderr and raises `SystemExit` on failure 72→from erk.cli.subprocess_utils import run_with_error_reporting 74→# ✅ CORRECT: User-friendly error messages + SystemExit 75→run_with_error_reporting( 84→- **User-friendly**: Error messages are clear and actionable 86→- **No exception handling needed**: Wrapper handles everything 90→- **Rich error messages**: Both wrappers include operation context, command, exit code, and stderr 91→- **Exception chaining**: Preserves original CalledProcessError for debugging 92→- **Consistent patterns**: Two clear boundaries with appropriate error handling 93→- **Debugging support**: Full context available in error messages and logs 110→Not all subprocess calls should use `run_with_error_reporting()`. Use explicit exception handling when: 112→1. **The operation is optional** - Failure should not stop the main workflow 114→3. **Warning vs Error** - You want to show a warning and continue, not exit 119→# Pattern: check=True with explicit CalledProcessError handling 123→except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e: 124→ # Show warning, continue execution 125→ user_output(click.style("⚠ ", fg="yellow") + f"Optional operation failed: {e}") 126→except FileNotFoundError: 135→| CLI command that must succeed | `run_with_error_reporting()` | SystemExit on failure is correct | 136→| Optional background operation | Explicit exception handling | Main operation should continue | 137→| Gateway real.py implementation | `run_subprocess_with_context()` | Consistent error wrapping | 141→For GitHub API commands that may fail due to transient network errors, use `execute_gh_command_with_retry()`: 151→- Automatic retry on transient errors (network timeouts, connection failures) 157→## Error Accumulation Pattern 183→# Use accumulated stderr in error msg 190→- Stderr is accumulated as list, joined only when needed for error message 259→| **Error handling** | Returns `None` on any failure | Raises exception or returns error type | 262→| **Caller responsibility** | Check for `None`, decide how to handle | Catch exception or check error type | 270→See `_get_pr_for_plan_direct()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/trigger_async_learn.py`. Returns `dict[str, object] | None` — `None` on any failure (missing issue, metadata, branch, or PR). 274→- **No exceptions** - Never raises, always returns `None` on failure 275→- **No error messages** - Caller decides what to log 276→- **Uniform failure** - All failure modes return `None` consistently 286→Use when the operation is **critical** and failure should be explicit: 288→See `get_pr_for_plan()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/get_pr_for_plan.py` for the full implementation. It is a Click command that raises `SystemExit(1)` with JSON error output on any failure, and attempts branch_name recovery via pattern matching. 292→- **Explicit errors** - Each failure mode has specific error message 294→- **Clear contract** - Caller knows exceptions mean critical failure 298→- User-facing commands where failure needs explanation 300→- CLI commands that should exit with error message 331→The same PR lookup in `get_pr_for_plan.py` is **strict** because it's a user-facing command where the user explicitly asked for the PR and expects either the answer or a clear error message. 367→- **CLI layer**: Use `run_with_error_reporting()` for command handlers
325: SUMMARY=$(erk exec summarize-impl-failure \

1→"""Bundled skill capabilities — cached factory. 2→ 3→All simple skill capabilities (those that only need a name and description) 4→are registered here. The dict is created lazily via a cached function to 5→avoid module-import-time allocation. 6→ 7→Skills with custom install/uninstall logic (like learned-docs) are NOT here; 8→they have their own capability classes. 9→""" 10→ 11→from functools import cache 12→ 13→from erk.core.capabilities.codex_portable import codex_portable_skills 14→from erk.core.capabilities.skill_capability import SkillCapability 15→from erk_shared.context.types import AgentBackend 16→ 17→_UNBUNDLED_SKILLS: frozenset[str] = frozenset( 18→ { 19→ "ci-iteration", 20→ "cli-skill-creator", 21→ "cmux", 22→ "command-creator", 23→ # npx-managed: as skills migrate to npx distribution, they move from 24→ # bundled_skills() to _UNBUNDLED_SKILLS and can be removed from 25→ # codex_portable_skills() and pyproject.toml force-include. 26→ "dignified-python", # npx-managed 27→ "fake-driven-testing", # npx-managed 28→ "learned-docs", # has its own capability class (LearnedDocsCapability) 29→ "npx-skills", 30→ "refac-cli-push-down",
... [55 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→name: erk-planning 3→description: "[REMOVED] This skill has been deleted. Plan management is now handled by slash commands and AGENTS.md." 4→--- 5→ 6→This skill has been removed. Use the following instead: 7→ 8→- `/erk:plan-save` — save plan as draft PR 9→- `/local:plan-update` — update an existing plan 10→- `/erk:replan` — replan against current codebase 11→- `AGENTS.md` — ambient planning workflow documentation 12→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>

1→"""Teleport a PR's remote state to the local branch. 2→ 3→Remote-first counterpart to 'checkout'. While checkout preserves local state 4→(reusing existing worktrees, keeping unpushed commits), teleport force-resets 5→the local branch to match the remote exactly. Use teleport when a remote agent 6→has pushed commits and you want your local copy to match. 7→ 8→Operates on the current worktree by default, with --new-slot to create a fresh 9→worktree slot. 10→""" 11→ 12→from contextlib import nullcontext 13→from pathlib import Path 14→ 15→import click 16→ 17→from erk.cli.activation import ( 18→ activation_config_activate_only, 19→ ensure_worktree_activate_script, 20→ print_activation_instructions, 21→) 22→from erk.cli.commands.checkout_helpers import ( 23→ ensure_branch_has_worktree, 24→ navigate_and_display_checkout, 25→ script_error_handler, 26→) 27→from erk.cli.commands.pr.checkout_cmd import _fetch_and_update_branch 28→from erk.cli.commands.slot.common import find_assignment_by_worktree, update_slot_assignment_tip 29→from erk.cli.ensure import Ensure 30→from erk.cli.help_formatter import CommandWithHiddenOptions, script_option
... [350 lines omitted] ...
69→ ctx.console.error("Not in a git repository") 74→ ctx_manager = script_error_handler(ctx) 82→ ctx.console.error( 89→ ctx.console.error("Teleport is not supported for cross-repository (fork) PRs.") 252→ # Check if branch already has a worktree — navigate to it instead of erroring 350→ except RuntimeError: 365→ click.style("Warning: ", fg="yellow")

session.tool_result:
1→"""Cancel a workflow run command.""" 2→ 3→import click 4→ 5→from erk.cli.core import discover_repo_context 6→from erk.cli.ensure import Ensure 7→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 8→from erk_shared.output.output import user_output 9→ 10→ 11→@click.command("cancel") 12→@click.argument("run_id") 13→@click.pass_obj 14→def cancel_run(ctx: ErkContext, run_id: str) -> None: 15→ """Cancel an in-progress or queued workflow run.""" 16→ Ensure.gh_authenticated(ctx) 17→ repo = discover_repo_context(ctx, ctx.cwd) 18→ 19→ ctx.github.cancel_workflow_run(repo.root, run_id) 20→ user_output(f"Cancelled workflow run {click.style(run_id, fg='cyan')}") 21→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>

1→"""Retry a workflow run command.""" 2→ 3→import click 4→ 5→from erk.cli.core import discover_repo_context 6→from erk.cli.ensure import Ensure 7→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 8→from erk_shared.output.output import user_output 9→ 10→ 11→@click.command("retry") 12→@click.argument("run_id") 13→@click.option("--failed", is_flag=True, help="Only re-run failed jobs") 14→@click.pass_obj 15→def retry_run(ctx: ErkContext, run_id: str, failed: bool) -> None: 16→ """Retry a completed workflow run.""" 17→ Ensure.gh_authenticated(ctx) 18→ repo = discover_repo_context(ctx, ctx.cwd) 19→ 20→ ctx.github.rerun_workflow_run(repo.root, run_id, failed_only=failed) 21→ label = "failed jobs of " if failed else "" 22→ user_output(f"Retried {label}workflow run {click.style(run_id, fg='cyan')}") 23→
<system-reminder> Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>

1→"""Fetch the activity log from an existing review summary comment. 2→ 3→This exec command finds the PR comment containing the given marker 4→and extracts the Activity Log section from it. 5→ 6→Usage: 7→ erk exec get-review-activity-log --pr-number 123 \\ 8→ --marker "<!-- audit-pr-docs -->" 9→ 10→Output: 11→ JSON with found status and activity log text 12→ 13→Exit Codes: 14→ 0: Always (even on error, to support || true pattern) 15→ 16→Examples: 17→ $ erk exec get-review-activity-log --pr-number 123 \\ 18→ --marker "<!-- audit-pr-docs -->" 19→ {"success": true, "found": true, "activity_log": "- [2024-01-01] ..."} 20→ 21→ $ erk exec get-review-activity-log --pr-number 123 \\ 22→ --marker "<!-- no-such-marker -->" 23→ {"success": true, "found": false, "activity_log": ""} 24→""" 25→ 26→import json 27→ 28→import click 29→ 30→from erk_shared.context.helpers import require_github, require_repo_root
... [56 lines omitted] ...

1→"""Sync all stack branches with their remote tracking branches.""" 2→ 3→import click 4→ 5→from erk.cli.core import discover_repo_context 6→from erk.cli.graphite_command import GraphiteCommand 7→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 8→from erk.core.stack_sync import BranchSyncAction, StackSyncResult, sync_stack 9→ 10→ 11→@click.command("sync", cls=GraphiteCommand, hidden=True) 12→@click.pass_obj 13→def sync_stack_cmd(ctx: ErkContext) -> None: 14→ """Sync all stack branches with their remote tracking branches. 15→ 16→ Fetches remote state and resolves divergences across the entire Graphite 17→ stack. For each non-trunk branch, checks if the remote has new commits 18→ and fast-forwards or rebases as needed. After syncing, restacks the 19→ entire stack. 20→ 21→ If a rebase has conflicts, aborts the rebase and suggests using 22→ 'erk pr diverge-fix' for that specific branch. 23→ """ 24→ repo = discover_repo_context(ctx, ctx.cwd) 25→ 26→ click.echo("Fetching remote state...") 27→ click.echo("") 28→ 29→ result = sync_stack(ctx, repo_root=repo.root, cwd=ctx.cwd) 30→
... [81 lines omitted] ...
31→ # Handle fatal errors (detached HEAD, not tracked) 32→ if not result.branch_results and result.restack_error is not None: 33→ click.echo(f"Error: {result.restack_error}", err=True) 65→ case BranchSyncAction.ERROR: 66→ return click.style(f"error: {detail}", fg="red") 75→ elif result.restack_error is not None: 76→ click.echo(f"Restacking... {click.style('failed', fg='red')}: {result.restack_error}")
